content.problemStatement:
Given a singly linked list, determine if it contains a cycle/loop. A loop exists when the last node's next pointer points to any previous node in the list instead of NULL.

Note: Update the `detectLoop` with your solution


Input Format

First line contains TTT, the number of test cases
For each test case:

First line contains NNN, the number of nodes
Second line contains NNN integers denoting the values of the nodes
Third line contains pos, the position (1-based) where the last node connects to (0 if no loop)




Output Format

For each test case, print "YES" if a loop exists, "NO" otherwise.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. For each test case: First line contains N, the number of nodes. Second line contains N integers denoting the values of the nodes. Third line contains pos, the position (1-based) where the last node connects to (0 if no loop).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print 'YES' if a loop exists, 'NO' otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: N=3, values [1,3,4], pos=2 → last node (4) points to node 2 (value 3) → loop → YES.
Test case 2: N=4, values [1,8,3,4], pos=0 → no connection from last node → no loop → NO.
Test case 3: N=4, values [1,2,3,4], pos=1 → last node points to the head → loop → YES.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm, also known as the 'tortoise and the hare' algorithm. This algorithm uses two pointers, one moving twice as fast as the other. If there is a cycle, these two pointers will eventually meet at some node within the cycle.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T*N) total. The algorithm runs in linear time because in the worst case, the fast pointer may need to traverse the entire linked list.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the algorithm only uses a constant amount of space to store the two pointers and does not use any data structures that scale with input size.
